title: Ulcerative Colitis
oneLiner: Chronic inflammatory bowel disease of the colorectal mucosa; relapsing-remitting; risk of life-threatening acute severe colitis in ~15% of patients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Toxic megacolon** — abdominal distension + tenderness + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). *A single AXR showing transverse colon >6 cm diameter = this is the killer, not routine colitis.*
- **Colonic perforation** — sudden severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, free air under diaphragm on erect CXR. *A rigid abdomen + hypotension = surgical emergency, not medical management.*
- **Massive haemorrhage** — large-volume haematochezia, haemodynamic instability, rapid drop in Hb. *Unstable patient with ongoing red blood per rectum = this is not just "a flare."*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** bloody diarrhoea (often with mucus), urgency, tenesmus, frequency of stools (≥6/day in severe disease), nocturnal symptoms (waking to defecate), colicky lower abdominal pain (often left-sided).
- **Key exam findings:** abdominal tenderness (usually left iliac fossa), distension (if megacolon), fever, tachycardia, pallor (if anaemic); perianal inspection usually normal (if fistulas/abscesses → think Crohn’s).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Infectious colitis (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, E. coli O157, *C. difficile*) — stool culture + C. diff toxin before steroids.
- **Less likely:** Crohn’s colitis — perianal disease + skip lesions + non-bloody diarrhoea more common; **splitter:** *Perianal fistula/abscess suggests Crohn’s, not UC.*
- **Other:** Ischaemic colitis (older, sudden onset, abdominal pain); diverticulitis (left lower quadrant pain, fever without bloody stool); NSAID-induced colitis (history); colorectal cancer (haematochezia + weight loss + iron deficiency anaemia).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Stool sample for MC&S, *C. difficile* toxin, and O&P (ova, cysts, parasites) — **never give steroids before stool cultures.** Check stool calprotectin (POCT if available) — supports inflammation.
- **Bloods:** FBC (Hb, platelet count — thrombocytosis often in active disease), U&E (K+ often low due to diarrhoea), CRP, ESR, albumin (low in severe disease), LFTs (baseline before immunomodulators). Blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First line (severe disease):** AXR (assess for megacolon → transverse colon >6 cm, thumbprinting, mucosal oedema).  
  - **Second line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (if suspicion of perforation or abscess) — avoid oral contrast if acute.  
  - **Erect CXR** if suspected perforation to check for free air.
- **Special tests:** Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsies — confirm diagnosis; avoid full colonoscopy in acute severe disease (risk of perforation).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Truelove & Witts' criteria for *acute severe colitis*: ≥6 bloody stools/day + any one of: HR >90, temp >37.8°C, Hb <105 g/L, ESR >30 mm/h, CRP >30 mg/L.  
  - *Mild:* <4 stools/day, no systemic upset.  
  - *Moderate:* 4–6 stools/day, minimal systemic upset.  
  - *Severe:* ≥6 stools/day + systemic features.
- **Never-miss test:** Stool MC&S + *C. difficile* toxin before starting steroids.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If severe (Truelove & Witts): IV fluids, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS, and VTE prophylaxis (LMWH).  
  - If toxic megacolon: NBM, NG tube, IV fluids, IV steroids, surgical consult.  
  - If perforation or massive haemorrhage: emergency surgical review, crossmatch, NBM, IV antibiotics.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV steroids for severe disease (hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS or methylprednisolone 60 mg daily).  
  - Monitor stool frequency, CRP, abdominal signs daily.  
  - Consider topical therapy if distal disease: mesalazine enema 1 g nocte or prednisolone foam enema.  
  - If no improvement after 3 days of IV steroids → CT scan to exclude perforation/abscess, then consider second-line (infliximab or ciclosporin) or surgical referral.  
  - Nutritional support: oral fluids/light diet if tolerated; TPN only if NBM >5 days.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Maintain remission: oral mesalazine (e.g. Asacol, Mezavant) 2.4–4.8 g/day.  
  - Thiopurines (azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day or mercaptopurine 1–1.5 mg/kg/day) for steroid-refractory/steroid-dependent disease.  
  - Biologics: infliximab [check local policy for dosing and scheduling] or adalimumab; vedolizumab for anti-TNF failures.  
  - Colorectal cancer surveillance: colonoscopy 8–10 years after diagnosis, then every 1–5 years depending on risk.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for severe colitis):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride + 20 mmol KCl per litre if K+ <3.5 (recheck U&E daily).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless high bleeding risk.  
  - Diet: NBM if suspected megacolon; otherwise light oral diet (avoid high-fibre).  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or more frequent if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (mild-moderate):**  
  - Mesalazine oral: Asacol 400 mg–800 mg TDS or Mezavant XL 2.4–4.8 g OD.  
  - Topical mesalazine enema: 1 g nocte (Salofalk, Pentasa).  
- **First-line (severe):**  
  - IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS (preferred) or IV methylprednisolone 60 mg OD × 3–5 days, then taper.  
  - If no response after 3 days → **infliximab 5 mg/kg IV** (single dose, then repeat at 2 and 6 weeks) - check local protocol for infusion.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Oral prednisolone 40 mg OD (taper over 8–12 weeks) for moderate disease.  
  - Ciclosporin 2–4 mg/kg/day IV (monitor BP, U&E, levels) — use only in expert hands.  
  - Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day PO (slow up-titration; check TPMT genotype first).  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - IV steroids: caution in active infection (screen stool first).  
  - Mesalazine: hypersensitivity (pancreatitis, pneumonitis, pericarditis) — stop if eosinophilia or cardiac symptoms.  
  - Azathioprine: avoid with allopurinol; check TPMT to reduce myelotoxicity.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Mesalazine: caution in moderate-severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) — reduce dose, monitor Cr.  
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in hepatic impairment; avoid in severe liver disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a full dose of azathioprine without checking TPMT status** → risk of life-threatening myelosuppression (pancytopenia).  
  - **Using loperamide or other antidiarrhoeals in active colitis** → risk of toxic megacolon (impairs motility and toxin clearance).  
  - **Underdosing steroids in acute severe colitis** (e.g. oral rather than IV) → progression to toxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Toxic megacolon, perforation, massive haemorrhage, CMV colitis (if steroid-refractory), venous thromboembolism (VTE — increased risk in active disease).  
- **Chronic:** Colorectal cancer (risk after 8–10 years), primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC — run LFTs every 6 months), osteopenia/osteoporosis (long-term steroids), iron deficiency anaemia, malnutrition.
sections.Prognosis:
- Relapsing-remitting course; for acute severe colitis, ~40% respond to IV steroids; of the non-responders, ~50% will require colectomy or escalate to biologics.  
- Overall colectomy rate in UC ~20% over 10 years. Colorectal cancer risk increases by ~0.5–1% per year after 10 years of disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a stool culture + C. diff before steroids** — missing an infection can be fatal.  
- **In severe colitis, get a plain AXR daily** to monitor colonic diameter — if >6 cm, call surgical team.  
- **Start VTE prophylaxis immediately** — UC carries a 2–3× increased risk of DVT/PE.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give antidiarrhoeals (loperamide, codeine)** in active colitis — precipitates toxic megacolon.  
- **Do NOT perform full colonoscopy in acute severe colitis** — high risk of perforation; flexible sigmoidoscopy only.  
- **Do NOT delay surgical consult** if patient fails to improve after 72 hours of IV steroids (or if any sign of megacolon).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal pain, increasing stool frequency (>8/day), fever, vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, new distension.  
- **Return if:** Blood in stool increasing or haemodynamic instability (feeling faint, fast heart rate).  
- **Follow-up:** GI clinic within 2 weeks for disease assessment and treatment plan; start mesalazine maintenance (if not already).  
- **Safety netting:** Advise to avoid NSAIDs and antidiarrhoeals; give contact number for IBD nurse or emergency department.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/consultant):**  
  - NEWS2 >5 (e.g. RR >25, HR >130, SBP <90, temp >39°C).  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or rising.  
  - Any sign of peritonism or abdominal distension consistent with megacolon.  
  - GCS <15 or altered mental status (think infection/steroid-induced psychosis).  
  - Failure to improve after 72 hours of IV steroids (persistent bloody diarrhoea, CRP not falling).  
- **Escalate to ICU:**  
  - Hypotension despite fluid resuscitation, rising lactate, or need for vasopressors.  
  - Proven or suspected toxic megacolon (especially if colonic diameter >6 cm on AXR).  
  - Need for endotracheal intubation (e.g. for perforation).  
- **Crash call:** Unresponsive patient, cardiac arrest, severe haemorrhage with ongoing instability.

rawMarkdown:
## Ulcerative Colitis
**One-liner:** Chronic inflammatory bowel disease of the colorectal mucosa; relapsing-remitting; risk of life-threatening acute severe colitis in ~15% of patients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Toxic megacolon** — abdominal distension + tenderness + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). *A single AXR showing transverse colon >6 cm diameter = this is the killer, not routine colitis.*
- **Colonic perforation** — sudden severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, free air under diaphragm on erect CXR. *A rigid abdomen + hypotension = surgical emergency, not medical management.*
- **Massive haemorrhage** — large-volume haematochezia, haemodynamic instability, rapid drop in Hb. *Unstable patient with ongoing red blood per rectum = this is not just "a flare."*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** bloody diarrhoea (often with mucus), urgency, tenesmus, frequency of stools (≥6/day in severe disease), nocturnal symptoms (waking to defecate), colicky lower abdominal pain (often left-sided).
- **Key exam findings:** abdominal tenderness (usually left iliac fossa), distension (if megacolon), fever, tachycardia, pallor (if anaemic); perianal inspection usually normal (if fistulas/abscesses → think Crohn’s).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Infectious colitis (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, E. coli O157, *C. difficile*) — stool culture + C. diff toxin before steroids.
- **Less likely:** Crohn’s colitis — perianal disease + skip lesions + non-bloody diarrhoea more common; **splitter:** *Perianal fistula/abscess suggests Crohn’s, not UC.*
- **Other:** Ischaemic colitis (older, sudden onset, abdominal pain); diverticulitis (left lower quadrant pain, fever without bloody stool); NSAID-induced colitis (history); colorectal cancer (haematochezia + weight loss + iron deficiency anaemia).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Stool sample for MC&S, *C. difficile* toxin, and O&P (ova, cysts, parasites) — **never give steroids before stool cultures.** Check stool calprotectin (POCT if available) — supports inflammation.
- **Bloods:** FBC (Hb, platelet count — thrombocytosis often in active disease), U&E (K+ often low due to diarrhoea), CRP, ESR, albumin (low in severe disease), LFTs (baseline before immunomodulators). Blood cultures if febrile.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First line (severe disease):** AXR (assess for megacolon → transverse colon >6 cm, thumbprinting, mucosal oedema).  
  - **Second line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (if suspicion of perforation or abscess) — avoid oral contrast if acute.  
  - **Erect CXR** if suspected perforation to check for free air.
- **Special tests:** Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsies — confirm diagnosis; avoid full colonoscopy in acute severe disease (risk of perforation).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Truelove & Witts' criteria for *acute severe colitis*: ≥6 bloody stools/day + any one of: HR >90, temp >37.8°C, Hb <105 g/L, ESR >30 mm/h, CRP >30 mg/L.  
  - *Mild:* <4 stools/day, no systemic upset.  
  - *Moderate:* 4–6 stools/day, minimal systemic upset.  
  - *Severe:* ≥6 stools/day + systemic features.
- **Never-miss test:** Stool MC&S + *C. difficile* toxin before starting steroids.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If severe (Truelove & Witts): IV fluids, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS, and VTE prophylaxis (LMWH).  
  - If toxic megacolon: NBM, NG tube, IV fluids, IV steroids, surgical consult.  
  - If perforation or massive haemorrhage: emergency surgical review, crossmatch, NBM, IV antibiotics.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV steroids for severe disease (hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS or methylprednisolone 60 mg daily).  
  - Monitor stool frequency, CRP, abdominal signs daily.  
  - Consider topical therapy if distal disease: mesalazine enema 1 g nocte or prednisolone foam enema.  
  - If no improvement after 3 days of IV steroids → CT scan to exclude perforation/abscess, then consider second-line (infliximab or ciclosporin) or surgical referral.  
  - Nutritional support: oral fluids/light diet if tolerated; TPN only if NBM >5 days.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Maintain remission: oral mesalazine (e.g. Asacol, Mezavant) 2.4–4.8 g/day.  
  - Thiopurines (azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day or mercaptopurine 1–1.5 mg/kg/day) for steroid-refractory/steroid-dependent disease.  
  - Biologics: infliximab [check local policy for dosing and scheduling] or adalimumab; vedolizumab for anti-TNF failures.  
  - Colorectal cancer surveillance: colonoscopy 8–10 years after diagnosis, then every 1–5 years depending on risk.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for severe colitis):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride + 20 mmol KCl per litre if K+ <3.5 (recheck U&E daily).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless high bleeding risk.  
  - Diet: NBM if suspected megacolon; otherwise light oral diet (avoid high-fibre).  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or more frequent if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (mild-moderate):**  
  - Mesalazine oral: Asacol 400 mg–800 mg TDS or Mezavant XL 2.4–4.8 g OD.  
  - Topical mesalazine enema: 1 g nocte (Salofalk, Pentasa).  
- **First-line (severe):**  
  - IV hydrocortisone 100 mg QDS (preferred) or IV methylprednisolone 60 mg OD × 3–5 days, then taper.  
  - If no response after 3 days → **infliximab 5 mg/kg IV** (single dose, then repeat at 2 and 6 weeks) - check local protocol for infusion.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Oral prednisolone 40 mg OD (taper over 8–12 weeks) for moderate disease.  
  - Ciclosporin 2–4 mg/kg/day IV (monitor BP, U&E, levels) — use only in expert hands.  
  - Azathioprine 2–2.5 mg/kg/day PO (slow up-titration; check TPMT genotype first).  
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**  
  - IV steroids: caution in active infection (screen stool first).  
  - Mesalazine: hypersensitivity (pancreatitis, pneumonitis, pericarditis) — stop if eosinophilia or cardiac symptoms.  
  - Azathioprine: avoid with allopurinol; check TPMT to reduce myelotoxicity.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Mesalazine: caution in moderate-severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) — reduce dose, monitor Cr.  
  - Azathioprine: reduce dose in hepatic impairment; avoid in severe liver disease.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving a full dose of azathioprine without checking TPMT status** → risk of life-threatening myelosuppression (pancytopenia).  
  - **Using loperamide or other antidiarrhoeals in active colitis** → risk of toxic megacolon (impairs motility and toxin clearance).  
  - **Underdosing steroids in acute severe colitis** (e.g. oral rather than IV) → progression to toxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Toxic megacolon, perforation, massive haemorrhage, CMV colitis (if steroid-refractory), venous thromboembolism (VTE — increased risk in active disease).  
- **Chronic:** Colorectal cancer (risk after 8–10 years), primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC — run LFTs every 6 months), osteopenia/osteoporosis (long-term steroids), iron deficiency anaemia, malnutrition.

### Prognosis
- Relapsing-remitting course; for acute severe colitis, ~40% respond to IV steroids; of the non-responders, ~50% will require colectomy or escalate to biologics.  
- Overall colectomy rate in UC ~20% over 10 years. Colorectal cancer risk increases by ~0.5–1% per year after 10 years of disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check a stool culture + C. diff before steroids** — missing an infection can be fatal.  
- **In severe colitis, get a plain AXR daily** to monitor colonic diameter — if >6 cm, call surgical team.  
- **Start VTE prophylaxis immediately** — UC carries a 2–3× increased risk of DVT/PE.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give antidiarrhoeals (loperamide, codeine)** in active colitis — precipitates toxic megacolon.  
- **Do NOT perform full colonoscopy in acute severe colitis** — high risk of perforation; flexible sigmoidoscopy only.  
- **Do NOT delay surgical consult** if patient fails to improve after 72 hours of IV steroids (or if any sign of megacolon).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal pain, increasing stool frequency (>8/day), fever, vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, new distension.  
- **Return if:** Blood in stool increasing or haemodynamic instability (feeling faint, fast heart rate).  
- **Follow-up:** GI clinic within 2 weeks for disease assessment and treatment plan; start mesalazine maintenance (if not already).  
- **Safety netting:** Advise to avoid NSAIDs and antidiarrhoeals; give contact number for IBD nurse or emergency department.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/consultant):**  
  - NEWS2 >5 (e.g. RR >25, HR >130, SBP <90, temp >39°C).  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or rising.  
  - Any sign of peritonism or abdominal distension consistent with megacolon.  
  - GCS <15 or altered mental status (think infection/steroid-induced psychosis).  
  - Failure to improve after 72 hours of IV steroids (persistent bloody diarrhoea, CRP not falling).  
- **Escalate to ICU:**  
  - Hypotension despite fluid resuscitation, rising lactate, or need for vasopressors.  
  - Proven or suspected toxic megacolon (especially if colonic diameter >6 cm on AXR).  
  - Need for endotracheal intubation (e.g. for perforation).  
- **Crash call:** Unresponsive patient, cardiac arrest, severe haemorrhage with ongoing instability.
